title: Division in contexts
description: Practice connecting real life contexts to division expressions.
topic_path: Get ready for multiplying and dividing fractions

assessment_items.question_content:
For a field trip, $54$ students will be split evenly into groups of $9$. 

**Which expression helps us find out how many groups of students will be on the field trip?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{54}$ students total.


And we know the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$:

There are $\purpleC9$ students in each group.

To find out how many groups of students there are, we can divide the $\greenD{54}$ students into groups of $\purpleC9$.

$\greenD{54}\div\purpleC9$

assessment_items.question_content:
Katie is making necklaces. Each necklace uses $5$ beads.  She has $25$ beads in total. 

**Which expression helps us find out how many necklaces Katie can make?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{25}$ beads total.


And we know the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$:

There are $\purpleC5$ beads in each necklace.

To find out how many necklaces Katie can make, we can divide $\greenD{25}$ beads into groups of $\purpleC5$.

$\greenD{25}\div\purpleC5$

assessment_items.question_content:
The pet store puts $2$ snakes in a tank. They have a total of $18$ snakes.

**Which expression helps us find out how many tanks of snakes there are?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{18}$ snakes total.


And we know the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$:

There are $\purpleC2$ snakes in each tank.

To find out how many tanks there are, we can divide $\greenD{18}$ snakes into groups of $\purpleC2$.

$\greenD{18}\div\purpleC2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Amy has $16$ video games. She puts them into stacks of $4$ games each.

**Which expression helps us find out how many stacks of games there are?**

hints:

There are $\greenD{16}$ video games total.


And we know the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$:

There are $\purpleC4$ games per stack.

To find out how many stacks there are, we can divide $\greenD{16}$ video games into groups of $\purpleC4$.

$\greenD{16}\div\purpleC4$

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex, Brian, and Marta split a box of $12$ cookies evenly.

**Which expression helps us find out how many cookies each friend will receive?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{12}$ cookies total.


And we know the $\blueD{\text{number of groups}}$:

There are $\blueD3$ friends.

To find out how many cookies each friend will receive, we can divide $\greenD{12}$ cookies into $\blueD3$ groups.

$\greenD{12}\div\blueD3$

Tom has $30$ books divided evenly into $6$ boxes.

There are $\blueD6$ boxes.

To find out how many books are in each box, we can divide the $\greenD{30}$ books into $\blueD6$ groups.

assessment_items.question_content:
Janet has $7$ albums. She divides $42$ pictures evenly between the albums.

**Which expression helps us find out how many pictures are in each album?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{42}$ pictures total.


And we know the $\blueD{\text{number of groups}}$:

There are $\blueD7$ albums.

To find out how many pictures are in each album, we can divide the $\greenD{42}$ pictures into $\blueD7$ groups.

$\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $42\div7$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$ can represent $\greenD{42}$ total items split evenly into $\blueD7$ groups.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{42}$ into $\blueD7$ equal groups?

###Steven

We want to find out how many candy bars are left, so we subtract $\greenD{42} - \maroonD7$.

$\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Julie

We want to find out how many jellybeans there are, so we multiply $\blueD{7} \times \maroonD{42}$.

$\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Leslie

We want to split the $\greenD{42}$ minutes evenly over $\blueD7$ days.  So, we divide $\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$.

$\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{42}\div\blueD7$ can solve the following context:

- Leslie ran for 42 minutes total over one week. If she ran the same number of minutes each day, how many minutes did Leslie run in one day?

choices.content: Leslie ran for $42$ minutes total in one week. If she ran the same number of minutes each day, how many minutes did Leslie run in one day?
Steven packed $42$ candy bars in his bag. He gave $7$ of them away to his friends. How many candy bars does Steven have left?
Julie had $7$ bags of jellybeans. There are $42$ jellybeans in each bag. How many jellybeans does Julie have?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $64\div8$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$ can represent $\greenD{64}$ total items split evenly into $\blueD8$ groups.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{64}$ into $\blueD8$ equal groups?

###Molly

We want to find out how many jellybeans are left, so we subtract $\greenD{64} - \maroonD8$.

$\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Daryl

We want to find out how many pencils there are, so we multiply $\greenD{64} \times \maroonD8$.

$\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Marco

We want to split the $\greenD{64}$ toys cars evenly into $\blueD8$ groups.  So, we divide $\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$.

$\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{64}\div\blueD8$ can solve the following context:

- Marco has $64$ toy cars. He divides them into $8$ equal groups. How many cars are in each group?

choices.content: Marco has $64$ toy cars. He divides them into $8$ equal groups. How many cars are in each group?
Molly has $64$ jellybeans. She eats $8$ of them. How many jellybeans does she have left?
Daryl has $8$ bins of pencils. There are $64$ pencils in each bin. How many pencils does Daryl have in all?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $32\div4$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$ can represent $\greenD{32}$ total items split evenly into $\blueD4$ groups.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{32}$ into $\blueD4$ equal groups?

###Chef

We want to find out how many plates are left, so we subtract $\greenD{32} - \maroonD4$.

$\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Fred

We want to find out how many carrots Fred has, so we multiply $\greenD{32} \times \maroonD4$.

$\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Art museum

We want to split the $\greenD{32}$ paintings evenly into $\blueD4$ rooms.  So, we divide $\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$.

$\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{32}\div\blueD4$ can solve the following context:

- An art museum has $4$ rooms. The museum owns $32$ paintings and has the same number in each room. How many paintings are in each room?

choices.content: An art museum has $4$ rooms. The museum has $32$ paintings and puts the same number of paintings in each room. How many paintings are in each room?
A chef needed to put $32$ plates away in the kitchen. She broke $4$ of the plates. How many plates does she have left?
Fred has $32$ rows of carrots. There are $4$ carrots in each row. How many carrots does Fred have in all?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $27\div3$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$ can represent $\greenD{27}$ total items split evenly into $\blueD3$ groups.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{27}$ into $\blueD3$ equal groups?

###Gino

We want to find out how many walnut trees are left, so we subtract $\greenD{27} - \maroonD3$.

$\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Lindsey

We want to find out how many apples there are, so we multiply $\greenD{27} \times \maroonD3$.

$\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###LaTasha

We want to split the $\greenD{27}$ stickers evenly onto $\blueD3$ pieces of paper.  So, we divide $\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$.

$\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{27}\div\blueD3$ can solve the following context:

- LaTasha has $27$ rabbit stickers. She splits the stickers evenly among $3$ pieces of paper. How many stickers did LaTasha put on each piece of paper?

choices.content: LaTasha has $27$ rabbit stickers. She splits the stickers evenly among $3$ pieces of paper. How many stickers did LaTasha put on each piece of paper?
Gino had $27$ walnut trees in his yard. He cut $3$ down to use for firewood. How many walnut trees does Gino have left?
Lindsey picked $3$ bags of apples. There are $27$ apples in each bag. How many apples does she have in total?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $63\div9$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$ can represent $\greenD{63}$ total items split evenly into groups of $\purpleC9$.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{63}$ into groups of $\purpleC9$?

###Jessica

We want to find out how many pieces of pizza are left, so we subtract $\greenD{63} - \maroonD9$.

$\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Tim

We want to find out how many scissors there are, so we add $\greenD{63} + \maroonD9$.

$\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Tatsu

We want to split the $\greenD{63}$ lemons evenly into groups of $\purpleC9$. So, we divide $\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$.

$\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{63}\div\purpleC9$ can solve the following context:

- Tatsu needs $9$ lemons to make a pitcher of lemonade. If Tatsu has $63$ lemons, how many pitchers of lemonade can he make?

choices.content: Tatsu needs $9$ lemons to make a pitcher of lemonade. If Tatsu has $63$ lemons, how many pitchers of lemonade can he make?
There are $63$ scissors in the drawer. Tim placed $9$ more scissors in the drawer. How many scissors are there in total?
Jessica has $63$ pieces of pizza. She ate $9$ pieces with her friends. How many pieces of pizza does she have left?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $60\div6$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$ can represent $\greenD{60}$ total items split evenly into groups of $\purpleC6$.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{60}$ into groups of $\purpleC6$?

###Millersburg bakery

We want to find out how many bags of sugar are left, so we subtract $\greenD{60} - \maroonD6$.

$\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Jeweler

We want to find out how many gems there are, so we add $\greenD{60} + \maroonD6$.

$\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Billy

We want to split the $\greenD{60}$ bikes evenly into groups of $\purpleC6$. So, we divide $\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$.

$\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{60}\div\purpleC6$ can solve the following context:

- There are $60$ bikes parked in the bike racks at Billy's school. If there are $6$ bikes parked at each bike rack, how many bike racks are there?

choices.content: There are $60$ bikes parked in the bike racks at Billy's school. If there are $6$ bikes parked at each bike rack, how many bike racks are there?
The Millersburg bakery had $60$ bags of sugar. They used $6$ bags of sugar to make some oatmeal cookies. How many bags of sugar are left?
A jeweler had $60$ gems. She bought $6$ more at an auction. How many gems does she have now?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which problem can we solve with $90\div10$?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
The expression $\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$ can represent $\greenD{90}$ total items split evenly into groups of $\purpleC{10}$.

Which problem wants to split $\greenD{90}$ into groups of $\purpleC{10}$?

###Jewelry store

We want to find out how many rings are left, so we subtract $\greenD{90} - \maroonD{10}$.

$\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Ernesto

We want to find out how many posters there are, so we add $\greenD{90} + \maroonD{10}$.

$\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$ does **not** help us solve this problem.

###Rachel

We want to split the $\greenD{90}$ notecards evenly into groups of $\purpleC{10}$. So, we divide $\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$.

$\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$ helps us solve this problem!

The expression $\greenD{90}\div\purpleC{10}$ can solve the following context:

- Rachel took $90$ notecards and arranged them in piles of $10$. How many piles did Rachel make?

choices.content: Rachel took $90$ notecards and arranged them in piles of $10$. How many piles did Rachel make?
A jewelry store has $90$ rings to display. They sold $10$ rings. How many rings do they have left?
Ernesto made $90$ posters. Donna made $10$ posters. How many posters do they have altogether?

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $20$ flowers. We want to divide them equally into $4$ vases. 

**What does the expression $20\div 4$ represent?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{20}$ flowers total.


And we know the $\blueD{\text{number of groups}}$:

There are $\blueD4$ vases.

The quotient will tell us the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$.

In this problem, the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$ is the $\purpleC{\text{number of flowers in each vase}}$.
The quotient tells us the $\purpleC{\text{number of flowers in each vase}}$.

choices.content: Number of flowers in each vase

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $56$ rooms in a hotel split evenly between $7$ floors.

**What does the expression $56\div7$ represent?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{56}$ hotel rooms.


And we know the $\blueD{\text{number of groups}}$:

There are $\blueD7$ floors.

The quotient will tell us the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$.

In this problem, the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$ is the $\purpleC{\text{number of rooms on each floor}}$.
The quotient tells us the $\purpleC{\text{number of rooms on each floor}}$.

choices.content: Number of rooms on each floor

The quotient tells us the $\purpleC{\text{number of ice cubes in each cup}}$.

hints:
We know the $\greenD{\text{total}}$:

There are $\greenD{24}$ lemons.


And we know the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$:
